verbatim: It saves me at least an hour or two per contract.
I treat it like a brilliant intern who has read every document in the world but has absolutely zero real-world business context or common sense.
theme: Mental model of AI as junior associate

verbatim: They might give me an answer that sounds incredibly authoritative, but could be fundamentally missing the business context, or worse, misinterpreting a subtle linguistic nuance in
Absolutely. Just last month, we were reviewing an updated cross-border data privacy addendum. The AI assured me that the indemnification cap was reciprocal.
That’s why the UX of these tools can be dangerous; they present information so beautifully and confidently that it coaxes you into letting your guard down.
theme: Risk perception due to tool's presentation

verbatim: The number one thing I need is verifiability at the point of consumption.

If an AI legal assistant writes, 'Under French law, you have 30 days to cure this specific breach,' that text is completely useless to me unless there is a hyperlinked footnote right next to it that hooks into the exact section of the French Civil Code.
theme: Need for inline citations and direct access to source material

verbatim: I want a literal confidence spectrum or clear visual indicators. If the tool is pulling from an ambiguous statute or conflicting case law, it shouldn't try to smooth it over with slick prose. It should highlight that section in amber or red and say, 'Warning: Case law on this specific point is split in the 9th Circuit. Click here to see the two competing precedents.'
theme: Need for clear indicators of AI's confidence level and uncertainty

verbatim: The current UX assumes that lawyers are prompt engineers. I don't want to type out a 500-word prompt every time I want the AI to look at a document. I need intuitive, structural building blocks—toggles, radio buttons, drop-down policies—where I can visually construct our legal posture without writing code or formatting perfect paragraphs.
theme: Need for an intuitive and user-friendly setup process

verbatim: On one hand, Sales loves it because they want their contracts approved yesterday. They constantly pressure us, saying, 'Can’t you just run this through the AI and sign it?' They see AI as a magic 'Go Fast' button.
theme: Client's desire for speed in contract approval

verbatim: Our Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) almost had a heart attack.
It’s a non-negotiable threshold: our data cannot be used to train any public or third-party models. Period. Everything must live in a completely isolated, single-tenant cloud environment.
I draw a hard line at autonomous execution or advice given directly to business units without a human lawyer in the loop.
That is highly problematic. It constitutes the unauthorized practice of law by a machine, and it bypasses the holistic risk management that only a human can provide.
Law isn't just binary logic; it’s a negotiation of human relationships and calculated gambles.
I would make them humble. The current UX paradigm of chat-based AI is built on the illusion of omniscience.
theme: Desire for transparency and humility in AI interfaces

verbatim: Give us control, give us transparency, and show us the receipts for every single claim the AI makes.
theme: Need for transparency and control in legal AI tools
